title: NOC 95102 – Labourers in chemical products processing and utilities | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s energy and utility sectors are at the heart of the national "Clean Power" transition. Labourers in Chemical Products Processing and Utilities (NOC 95102) provide the vital manual support required to operate refineries, water treatment plants, and pharmaceutical facilities. While classified as TEER 5, these roles are essential to maintaining Canada’s critical infrastructure. For international workers, this NOC offers a unique entry point into high-stability utility sectors with clear "internal-track" pathways to becoming certified plant operators.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Machine Feeding: Loading and unloading production machinery and equipment with chemical raw materials.
Facility Maintenance: Cleaning chemical processing machines, tanks, and production areas to ensure strict safety and environmental compliance.
Operational Support: Assisting operators to maintain process equipment, gas distribution lines, and water filtration systems.
Material Logistics: Moving, sorting, and piling chemical products manually or using powered equipment like forklifts.
2026 Update: Assisting in the maintenance of carbon-capture units and high-efficiency water recycling systems.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education may be required; a high school diploma is preferred by 80% of employers for long-term progression.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided. No previous experience is mandatory, making it an excellent "first job" in Canada.
Physical Demands: Ability to stand for long periods and handle materials manually (up to 20kg).
Certifications: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS certification. Many employers require specialized "Process Safety Management" (PSM) orientation upon hiring.
salary_prospects: Note: In Ontario's Windsor-Sarnia region, median wages often reach $33.73/hr due to the high concentration of petrochemical industry seniority.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates a "Balanced" market with significant retirement openings. As Canada’s population grows, the demand for water treatment and waste management services—and the labourers who maintain them—is projected to increase by 12% by 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for chemical and utility labourers is driven by infrastructure upgrades and the pharmaceutical boom:
Ontario (Windsor-Sarnia & GTA): High demand. Sarnia’s "Chemical Valley" remains a top employer, while the GTA sees growth in pharmaceutical and water treatment utility roles.
Alberta: Very Good prospects. Specifically in the Edmonton and Fort McMurray regions to support the evolving petrochemical and hydrogen production sectors.
British Columbia: Good prospects. Demand is high in the Mainland/Southwest region for water and waste treatment services supporting urban growth.
Saskatchewan: Stable demand. Focused on the chemical processing plants supporting the province's massive potash and fertilizer exports.
Why Work in Canada as a Chemical/Utility Labourer?
Essential Service Stability: Utilities and chemical processing are often recession-proof industries with long-term job security.
High-Tech Exposure: Even at an entry level, you will work with 2026-grade filtration, gas distribution, and waste management systems.
Top-Tier Benefits: Over 82% of workers in this NOC receive comprehensive employer-sponsored dental, medical, and pension plans.
Accelerated PR Potential: As "Essential Workers," these labourers are prime candidates for the 2026 TR to PR Pathway and various rural pilots.
pathways: 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A one-time 2026 initiative for 33,000 temporary workers. Labourers in utilities are prioritized under the "Essential Infrastructure" criteria.
Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream: NOC 95102 is a core eligible occupation for provincial nomination for those with a job offer in the province.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): High-success pathway in regional utility hubs where communities are actively recruiting for essential plant roles.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: One of the most direct PR pathways for TEER 5 labourers in the chemical and fertilizer sectors.
